topic: Peace, War & Neutrality: Britain, Northern Ireland & Ireland in WWII
subject: history

items.q: Did Irish neutrality mean Éire favoured Germany?
items.correct: Éire was officially neutral but leaned towards the Allies in practice — sharing weather reports, quietly returning Allied airmen and allowing the Donegal air corridor.
items.misconception: De Valera's neutrality meant Ireland secretly supported the Nazis.
items.random_wrong: Éire declared war on Germany in 1944.
items.why: Students read neutrality as hostility to Britain; historians describe Irish policy as 'benevolent neutrality' towards the Allies.

items.q: Why was Belfast so badly hit in the 1941 Blitz?
items.correct: Belfast was poorly prepared — few anti-aircraft guns, few shelters and little evacuation — because it was wrongly assumed to be beyond effective German bomber range.
items.misconception: Belfast escaped serious bombing because it was too far from Germany.
items.random_wrong: Belfast was never bombed during the Second World War.
items.why: Students assume distance equalled safety; the April 1941 raid killed around 900 people in one night, among the worst outside London.

items.q: Did Northern Ireland matter to the Allied war effort?
items.correct: Yes — its shipyards, aircraft factories, food exports and Atlantic bases, plus the arrival of US troops from 1942, made it strategically significant.
items.misconception: Northern Ireland sat out the war and contributed little because conscription was never applied there.
items.random_wrong: Northern Ireland was occupied by Germany for most of the war.
items.why: Students conflate no conscription with no contribution; voluntary service and industrial output were substantial.

items.correct: Éire's official term for the Second World War period, during which it remained neutral
items.misconception: The evacuation of London children to Wales
items.why: It's Éire's official term for the Second World War period, during which it remained neutral. “The evacuation of London children to Wales” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: When did the Luftwaffe heavily bomb Belfast?
items.why: It's April and May 1941. “September 1939” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why was Northern Ireland strategically important in the Battle of the Atlantic?
items.correct: Its ports and airfields helped protect convoys, especially after the Treaty Ports were returned to Éire in 1938
items.misconception: It was the only part of the UK with an army
items.why: Correct answer: Its ports and airfields helped protect convoys, especially after the Treaty Ports were returned to Éire in 1938. “It was the only part of the UK with an army” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Was conscription introduced in Northern Ireland during the war?
items.correct: No — it was never extended there, partly because of expected nationalist opposition
items.misconception: Yes — from September 1939, as in the rest of the UK
items.why: The answer examiners want is: No — it was never extended there, partly because of expected nationalist opposition. “Yes — from September 1939, as in the rest of the UK” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Belfast Blitz?
items.why: Exact answer: 1941 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
